---
title: "Aggregations API"
subtitle: "The Feature class for declarative time-windowed aggregations"
---

## Overview

The Aggregations API provides a declarative way to define time-windowed features using the `Feature` class. Instead of writing complex SQL with window functions, you specify what aggregations you want and the Feature Store handles the efficient computation.

::: {.callout-note}
## 🆕 New in snowflake-ml 1.21+
The Feature class and tiled aggregation are new capabilities requiring `snowflake-ml-python >= 1.21.0`.
:::

## Learning Objectives

After completing this chapter, you will be able to:

- Use the Feature class to define time-windowed aggregations
- Understand tiling for efficient incremental computation
- Configure feature granularity and refresh frequencies
- Apply feature column prefixing for disambiguation
- Name aggregated features following best practices

---

## The Feature Class

### Basic Usage

> 📁 **Full code:** [`_code/feature_class.py`](_code/feature_class.py)

```python
from snowflake.ml.feature_store import Feature, FeatureView

# Define time-windowed aggregations declaratively
features = [
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "7d").alias("TOTAL_SPEND_7D"),
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "30d").alias("TOTAL_SPEND_30D"),
    Feature.count("ORDER_ID", "7d").alias("ORDER_CNT_7D"),
    Feature.avg("AMOUNT", "7d").alias("AVG_ORDER_7D"),
    Feature.max("AMOUNT", "30d").alias("MAX_ORDER_30D"),
]

# Create tiled FeatureView
user_agg_fv = FeatureView(
    name="USER_PURCHASE_AGGREGATES",
    entities=[user_entity],
    feature_df=session.table("ORDERS"),
    timestamp_col="ORDER_TS",
    refresh_freq="1 hour",
    feature_granularity="1 hour",  # Tile size
    features=features,
)
```

### Available Aggregation Functions

| Function | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| `Feature.sum(col, window)` | Sum over window | `Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "7d")` |
| `Feature.count(col, window)` | Count over window | `Feature.count("ORDER_ID", "30d")` |
| `Feature.avg(col, window)` | Average over window | `Feature.avg("AMOUNT", "7d")` |
| `Feature.min(col, window)` | Minimum over window | `Feature.min("AMOUNT", "30d")` |
| `Feature.max(col, window)` | Maximum over window | `Feature.max("AMOUNT", "30d")` |
| `Feature.std(col, window)` | Standard deviation | `Feature.std("AMOUNT", "30d")` |
| `Feature.var(col, window)` | Variance | `Feature.var("AMOUNT", "30d")` |
| `Feature.approx_count_distinct(col, window)` | Approximate distinct count | `Feature.approx_count_distinct("PRODUCT_ID", "7d")` |
| `Feature.last_n(col, window, n)` | Last N values | `Feature.last_n("PRODUCT_ID", "7d", n=5)` |
| `Feature.first_n(col, window, n)` | First N values | `Feature.first_n("PRODUCT_ID", "7d", n=5)` |

### Window Specifications

Windows can be specified using shorthand notation:

| Notation | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `"1h"` or `"1 hour"` | 1 hour |
| `"24h"` | 24 hours |
| `"7d"` or `"7 day"` | 7 days |
| `"30d"` | 30 days |
| `"1w"` or `"1 week"` | 1 week |

---

## Naming Aggregated Features {#sec-naming}

### Best Practice: Descriptive Aliases

Always use `.alias()` to give features meaningful names:

```python
# ✅ GOOD: Descriptive names with window suffix
features = [
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "7d").alias("TOTAL_SPEND_7D"),
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "30d").alias("TOTAL_SPEND_30D"),
    Feature.count("ORDER_ID", "7d").alias("ORDER_CNT_7D"),
]

# ❌ BAD: Auto-generated names are hard to interpret
features = [
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "7d"),  # Name will be SUM_AMOUNT_7D
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "30d"),
]
```

---

## Feature Column Prefixing for Disambiguation {#sec-prefixing}

When joining features from multiple FeatureViews, column name collisions can occur. Feature Store provides two mechanisms for disambiguation.

### The Problem

```python
# Both FeatureViews have ORDER_CNT_7D
user_orders_fv = FeatureView(
    name="USER_ORDERS",
    features=[Feature.count("ORDER_ID", "7d").alias("ORDER_CNT_7D")],
    # ...
)

user_sessions_fv = FeatureView(
    name="USER_SESSIONS", 
    features=[Feature.count("SESSION_ID", "7d").alias("ORDER_CNT_7D")],  # Same name!
    # ...
)

# Without prefixing, collision occurs
dataset = fs.generate_dataset(
    spine_df=spine,
    features=[user_orders_fv, user_sessions_fv],  # Which ORDER_CNT_7D?
)
```

### Solution 1: Auto-Prefix

Use `auto_prefix=True` to automatically prefix columns with FeatureView name:

> 📁 **Full code:** [`_code/prefixing.py`](_code/prefixing.py)

```python
dataset = fs.generate_dataset(
    spine_df=spine,
    features=[user_orders_fv, user_sessions_fv],
    auto_prefix=True,  # Columns become USER_ORDERS__ORDER_CNT_7D, etc.
)

# Result columns:
# - USER_ORDERS__ORDER_CNT_7D
# - USER_SESSIONS__ORDER_CNT_7D
```

### Solution 2: Custom Prefixes with `.with_name()`

For more control, use `.with_name()` on individual FeatureViews or slices:

```python
dataset = fs.generate_dataset(
    spine_df=spine,
    features=[
        user_orders_fv.with_name("orders"),      # Prefix: orders__
        user_sessions_fv.with_name("sessions"),  # Prefix: sessions__
    ],
)

# Result columns:
# - orders__ORDER_CNT_7D
# - sessions__ORDER_CNT_7D
```

### Solution 3: Combining Both Approaches

```python
dataset = fs.generate_dataset(
    spine_df=spine,
    features=[
        user_orders_fv.with_name("orders"),  # Custom prefix
        user_sessions_fv,                     # Will use auto_prefix
    ],
    auto_prefix=True,
)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Priority
`.with_name()` takes precedence over `auto_prefix`. If a FeatureView has `.with_name()` set, that prefix is used regardless of `auto_prefix` setting.
:::

### Choosing a Prefixing Strategy

| Scenario | Recommendation |
|----------|----------------|
| Single FeatureView | No prefix needed |
| Multiple FVs, unique column names | No prefix needed |
| Multiple FVs, potential collisions | Use `auto_prefix=True` |
| Want short, custom prefixes | Use `.with_name()` |
| Consistent naming across projects | Define naming convention + `.with_name()` |

---

## Feature Granularity (Tiling) {#sec-tiling}

### How Tiling Works

The `feature_granularity` parameter sets the tile size for incremental computation:

```python
# Hourly tiles for high-frequency data
fv = FeatureView(
    feature_granularity="1 hour",  # Each tile covers 1 hour
    refresh_freq="15 minutes",      # Refresh every 15 minutes
    # ...
)
```

### Tile Size Recommendations

| Data Pattern | Recommended Tile | Reason |
|--------------|------------------|--------|
| Clickstream (high frequency) | `1 hour` | Many events per hour |
| Transactions (medium) | `1 day` | Daily aggregation patterns |
| Weekly metrics | `1 week` | Coarse granularity sufficient |

---

## Multi-Window Features

Define features with multiple time windows efficiently:

```python
features = [
    # Same metric, different windows
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "1d").alias("SPEND_1D"),
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "7d").alias("SPEND_7D"),
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "30d").alias("SPEND_30D"),
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "90d").alias("SPEND_90D"),
    
    # Useful for trend detection
    Feature.count("ORDER_ID", "7d").alias("ORDERS_7D"),
    Feature.count("ORDER_ID", "30d").alias("ORDERS_30D"),
]

# Single FeatureView efficiently computes all windows
multi_window_fv = FeatureView(
    name="USER_MULTI_WINDOW",
    features=features,
    feature_granularity="1 day",
    # ...
)
```

---

## Best Practices

### 1. Use Consistent Naming Conventions

```python
# Pattern: <METRIC>_<WINDOW>
features = [
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "7d").alias("TOTAL_SPEND_7D"),
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "30d").alias("TOTAL_SPEND_30D"),
    Feature.count("ORDER_ID", "7d").alias("ORDER_CNT_7D"),
    Feature.avg("AMOUNT", "7d").alias("AVG_ORDER_AMT_7D"),
]
```

### 2. Match Tile Size to Data Frequency

```python
# High-frequency: smaller tiles
clickstream_fv = FeatureView(
    feature_granularity="1 hour",
    # ...
)

# Low-frequency: larger tiles
weekly_metrics_fv = FeatureView(
    feature_granularity="1 day",
    # ...
)
```

### 3. Group Related Features

```python
# ✅ GOOD: Related features in same FeatureView
purchase_features = [
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "7d").alias("SPEND_7D"),
    Feature.count("ORDER_ID", "7d").alias("ORDERS_7D"),
    Feature.avg("AMOUNT", "7d").alias("AVG_ORDER_7D"),
]

# ❌ BAD: Unrelated features mixed together
mixed_features = [
    Feature.sum("AMOUNT", "7d"),  # Purchase feature
    Feature.count("LOGIN_ID", "7d"),  # Session feature (different entity!)
]
```

---

## Common Pitfalls

### ❌ Pitfall 1: No Alias

**Problem**: Auto-generated names are unclear.

**Solution**: Always use `.alias()` with descriptive names.

### ❌ Pitfall 2: Wrong Tile Size

**Problem**: Tile size doesn't match data frequency.

**Solution**: Use smaller tiles for high-frequency data.

### ❌ Pitfall 3: Column Collisions

**Problem**: Same column names from different FeatureViews.

**Solution**: Use `auto_prefix=True` or `.with_name()`.

---

## Summary

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Feature class** | Declarative API for time-windowed aggregations |
| **Window** | Time period for aggregation (e.g., "7d", "30d") |
| **Tile (feature_granularity)** | Pre-computation unit for efficiency |
| **auto_prefix** | Automatic column prefixing with FV name |
| `.with_name()` | Custom column prefixing |

---

## Next Steps

Continue to [Chapter 08: Online Features](../08_online_features/index.qmd) to learn about Online Feature Tables for real-time serving.
